title: Add CSI binary frame serialization host test
description:
Part of split of bf-brz. Depends on the nvs test child (bf-31q).

Goal: add a host-based unit test for the 24-byte binary CSI frame serialization.

Context (important, corrects the parent bead): the 24-byte frame is NOT packed in csi.c. It is packed in firmware/main/websocket.c inside the websocket_send_csi() function (around lines 226-262). csi.c only collects CSI and then calls websocket_send_csi. The header layout is: bytes 0-5 node_mac, 6-11 peer_mac, 12-19 timestamp_us (little-endian via memcpy), 20 rssi, 21 noise_floor, 22 channel, 23 n_sub, then n_sub*2 bytes of I/Q payload. SPAXEL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE is 24. This layout is mirrored by the Go parser in mothership/internal/ingestion/ -- cross-check against it.

Approach:
- websocket_send_csi() is coupled to the websocket client handle, the connection flag, and the send mutex, so it cannot be compiled on a host as-is. Extract just the header and payload packing into a pure host-compilable function (e.g. csi_frame_pack(out, node_mac, peer_mac, timestamp_us, rssi, noise_floor, channel, iq_data, n_sub) returning frame_len) with byte-identical output. Keep production websocket.c behavior identical (call the extracted packer from websocket_send_csi).
- Add a little-endian frame round-trip test asserting every header field at its exact byte offset, including: timestamp_us round-trips correctly (multi-byte LE), rssi/noise_floor sign handling (int8 as uint8), n_sub, and the I/Q payload bytes. Where helpful, validate packed bytes against the layout the Go ingestion parser expects.

Acceptance criteria:
- A test under firmware/test/ does at least one little-endian frame round-trip including all header fields.
- Test runs under the harness single documented command and passes.
- firmware/main/ production behavior is unchanged (extraction is refactor-only) and the firmware build still succeeds.

title: Add main() entry-point skeleton with setjmp recovery loop and run-command header
description:
Child 1 of the bf-bq9 sub-split (parent bf-bq9; grandparent bf-2i4; great-grandparent bf-56v; great-great-grandparent bf-4ne). Depends on sibling bf-3id (per-test failure recovery: g_test_jmp + g_failure_count + test_record_failure, already landed). This child is the entry-point core of the runner — the piece that makes a failing assertion non-fatal.

Scope (extend firmware/test/test_runner.c):
- Add a comment block above main() naming the documented run command: `make -C firmware/test test` (per the bf-1xs header contract and the bf-56v gcc-harness decision record). make compiles every test_*.c plus this runner with plain gcc and runs the binary; main()'s exit code is what make propagates.
- Implement int main(void): iterate the registry g_tests[] (the TEST() constructors have already fully populated it before main runs). For each test, setjmp(g_test_jmp) — the per-test target from sibling bf-3id — then call g_tests[i].fn(). On the direct setjmp return (0) the body ran clean: print "PASS: <name>" and bump a local passed counter. On a longjmp return (non-zero) a failed assertion tripped test_record_failure(): print "FAIL: <name>" and bump a local failed counter. Either way advance to the next test, so one test's failure never blocks the rest.
- For now return 0 — the non-zero-on-failure exit code is a later child.

Acceptance criteria:
- firmware/test/test_runner.c has an int main(void) that drives every registered test through the per-test setjmp/longjmp recovery loop, printing one PASS/FAIL line per test.
- A failing assertion in one test does NOT prevent later tests from running (the per-test setjmp/longjmp).
- A comment block above main() documents the run command `make -C firmware/test test`.
- Compiles cleanly with gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra; with at least one TEST() defined it links, runs, and exits 0 when all tests pass.
- No changes to firmware/main/* or firmware/CMakeLists.txt.

title: Add firmware/test/ harness header with TEST() macro and assert macros
description:
Child 1 of the bf-56v harness split (parent umbrella bf-56v; grandparent bf-4ne). Build ONLY the public header API that later children compile against.

Scope: create firmware/test/test_runner.h. It defines the harness API used by every test unit and by the runner:
- TEST(name): a macro that declares a void test function AND a GCC constructor (__attribute__((constructor))) that self-registers it via test_register(name, fn) before main() runs. This is what lets a new test_*.c be picked up with zero SOURCES edits.
- ASSERT_* macros (at minimum ASSERT_EQ(actual, expected)) that, on mismatch, call test_record_failure(file, line, fmt...) which longjmp()s back to a per-test setjmp — so a failing assertion aborts only the current test, not the whole suite.
- Prototypes for test_register(const char*, test_fn) and test_record_failure(...), and any jmp_buf plumbing needed (the live jmp target lives in test_runner.c, child 2).
- A header comment block that (a) records the gcc host harness decision from bf-21t (why gcc, not ESP-IDF --target linux), and (b) names the single documented run command that child 3 will make real: 'make -C firmware/test test'.

Acceptance criteria:
- firmware/test/test_runner.h exists and is self-contained (no firmware/main/* includes; only libc).
- TEST(name) self-registers via a constructor; a trivial includer .c using TEST(x){} compiles cleanly with 'gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -c'.
- ASSERT_EQ exists and routes failure through test_record_failure (no direct abort/exit).
- No main() function in the header.
- Header comment documents the bf-21t decision and the planned single run command.
- No changes to firmware/main/* or firmware/CMakeLists.txt.

title: Wrap each test body in a setjmp(g_test_jmp) guard so it runs only on the direct return
description:
Split-child 1 of 3 for parent bf-53ut (which is itself split-child 2 of 3 for bf-1na). This child is the actual code change that delivers the parent's core contract: drive each test through the per-test setjmp/longjmp recovery loop.

Starting state (from bf-1zew, parent bf-1na's child 1): main()'s per-test loop is the naive iteration that prints the RUN line then calls the body directly:

    for (int i = 0; i < g_test_count; i++) {
        printf("RUN: %s\n", g_tests[i].name);
        g_tests[i].fn();
    }

Scope (firmware/test/test_runner.c, main()'s per-test loop only):
- Before invoking the body, establish the per-test longjmp target by calling setjmp(g_test_jmp) — the file-scope jmp_buf added by sibling bf-3id. Gate the body call on its return: on the DIRECT call setjmp returns 0 and the body runs; replace the bare g_tests[i].fn() with `if (setjmp(g_test_jmp) == 0) { g_tests[i].fn(); }`.
- Keep the existing RUN: printf BEFORE the setjmp (do not move or reorder it) so the one observable line per test still prints regardless of how the body ends.
- Do NOT add outcome labels (PASS/FAIL) or any failure/pass tally counter here — those land in bf-1na's child 3, which is explicitly out of scope for this lineage. This child only wires the recovery target and gates the body on the 0 return; the non-zero path simply falls through (no else branch, or an empty/comment-only one).
- The loop's for-increment advances i either way, so a failure in test N never blocks N+1..end.

Acceptance criteria:
- main()'s per-test loop calls g_tests[i].fn() only inside `if (setjmp(g_test_jmp) == 0)`; the RUN: line still prints before the setjmp.
- Compiles cleanly with gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra (no setjmp-related warnings such as -Wclobbered).
- With an all-passing suite, `make -C firmware/test test` still runs every test and the process completes (behavior unchanged for passing tests).

title: Wrap the test body call in a setjmp(g_test_jmp) == 0 guard
description:
Split-child 1 of 3 for parent bf-53ut (Wrap each test in per-test setjmp/longjmp recovery so a failing assertion is non-fatal). This child installs the structural skeleton of the per-test recovery loop: it puts a setjmp(g_test_jmp) guard in front of the existing direct body call landed by sibling bf-1zew (run-command comment + naive registry iteration calling each g_tests[i].fn() directly), so that control returns here — rather than aborting — when a failed assertion inside the body calls test_record_failure() (which longjmp(g_test_jmp, 1) — sibling bf-3id).

Scope (extend firmware/test/test_runner.c, main()'s per-test loop):
- In main()'s loop, replace the bare g_tests[i].fn(); call with a guarded form: if (setjmp(g_test_jmp) == 0) { g_tests[i].fn(); }. On the DIRECT call setjmp returns 0 and the body runs as before; if the body trips test_record_failure(), the longjmp returns control to the setjmp call site with a non-zero value instead.
- On the non-zero (longjmp) return, do NOT re-invoke the body — leave the else branch empty (a bare comment is fine) for now. Just fall through so the loop advances to i+1. (Observability on the failure path lands in child 2; outcome PASS/FAIL labels land in a later sibling — do not add either here.)
- Either way the loop advances to the next i, so a failure in test N never blocks tests N+1..end. That per-test isolation is the whole point.

Implementation note (the classic setjmp trap — C11 7.13.2.1): any automatic variable modified between setjmp and longjmp AND read after the longjmp returns must be volatile-qualified, else its value is indeterminate. The loop index i is read in the post-longjmp path; confirm it is NOT modified between the setjmp call and the longjmp (it is only incremented by the for-loop increment, which runs AFTER control returns), so no volatile is needed. Record this reasoning in a comment next to the setjmp.

Acceptance criteria:
- main()'s loop calls setjmp(g_test_jmp) before each body and invokes g_tests[i].fn() ONLY on the 0 return; on a longjmp return it advances without re-calling.
- A comment near the setjmp records the volatile-analysis (i is not modified between setjmp and longjmp, so no volatile is needed).
- Compiles cleanly with gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra (no setjmp-related warnings).
- No changes to firmware/main/* or firmware/CMakeLists.txt.

The setjmp guard itself was already committed by an earlier single-pass bead (bf-bq9, in the file header), so this bead's incremental contribution is the volatile comment documenting that loop index i — read post-longjmp — is never modified between setjmp and longjmp (body only reads i; sole write is the for-loop increment after control returns), hence no volatile needed. Did NOT regress the pre-existing else-branch FAIL print, run summary, or non-zero exit code to a bare skeleton — those are committed bf-bq9 functionality the file header documents as the CI contract (make -C firmware/test test propagates non-zero on failure). title: Make the setjmp guard compile warning-free under gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra
description:
Split-child 3 of 4 for parent bf-22vg. The compile-cleanliness gate. setjmp inside a loop with automatic variables (the loop index i, and any other automatic that spans the setjmp/longjmp) routinely trips gcc -Wclobbered under -Wall -Wextra. The parent acceptance criteria require zero setjmp-related warnings, and this is the most common reason a setjmp/longjmp change fails to land. Depends on child 2 (the guard must exist first).

Scope: firmware/test/test_runner.c, only what is needed to clear setjmp warnings in/around main() per-test loop. No behavior change.

Do:
- Compile firmware/test/test_runner.c (and the test build) with gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra and confirm there are no -Wclobbered or other setjmp-related warnings.
- If -Wclobbered fires (typically on the loop index i or an automatic read after a possible longjmp return), fix it correctly per C11 7.13.2.1: volatile-qualify the affected automatic, or restructure so no non-volatile automatic is both modified before a possible longjmp and read after it. Do NOT silence the warning with a pragma or by downgrading flags.

Do NOT (out of scope):
- Change the guard logic itself (child 2), add outcome plumbing (sibling), or touch other modules.

Acceptance criteria:
- gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra produces zero warnings (in particular no -Wclobbered) on firmware/test/test_runner.c.
- Any volatile-qualification added is genuinely required and accompanied by a one-line rationale comment; no pragma/flag suppression.
- make -C firmware/test test still builds and runs.
- No changes to firmware/main/* or firmware/CMakeLists.txt.

title: Verify no loop variable is clobbered across the setjmp/longjmp boundary and the file compiles clean
description:
Split-child 2 of 3 for parent bf-53ut. Depends on child 1 (bf-22vg, which added the `if (setjmp(g_test_jmp) == 0) { g_tests[i].fn(); }` guard). This child owns the implementation-note correctness concern for the parent: the classic setjmp volatile trap (C11 7.13.2.1).

Background: any automatic variable that is modified between setjmp and longjmp AND read after the longjmp returns has an indeterminate value unless it is volatile-qualified. Getting this wrong is silent corruption — the value of `i` or a counter read in the post-longjmp path could be garbage. This child confirms the structure child 1 wrote is safe, and locks it in with a documenting comment so a future editor cannot regress it.

Scope (firmware/test/test_runner.c, the per-test loop region):
- Audit every automatic variable in scope across the setjmp/longjmp boundary in main()'s loop: the loop index `i`, and any local tallies (e.g. passed/failed if present). For each, confirm it is NOT both (a) written between the setjmp() call and a possible longjmp() fired inside the body AND (b) read in the post-longjmp path. The expected finding: `i` is written only by the for-loop increment, which runs AFTER control returns to the setjmp site (whether via normal return or longjmp), and the post-longjmp path does not read `i`; therefore no volatile qualifier is needed.
- Add a concise comment at the setjmp call site recording this analysis (variables considered, why each is safe, the C11 7.13.2.1 reference) so the reasoning is preserved with the code.
- Verify gcc actually agrees: build with gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra and confirm there is NO -Wclobbered warning (that is the diagnostic that fires when a non-volatile automatic could be clobbered). If any -Wclobbered appears, resolve it by either restructuring so the variable is not read post-longjmp, or volatile-qualifying the variable — then re-confirm a clean build.

Acceptance criteria:
- A comment at the setjmp call site documents the volatile/clobber analysis with the C11 7.13.2.1 reference and the list of variables checked.
- gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra emits NO -Wclobbered (and no other setjmp-related) warning for test_runner.c.
- No behavior change vs child 1 (this is analysis + comment + build verification only); `make -C firmware/test test` still completes on an all-passing suite.
- No changes to firmware/main/* or firmware/CMakeLists.txt.

title: Add auth token verification fuzz test
description: Plan target #3 (docs/plan/plan.md, Testing Strategy). Fuzz test for HMAC node-token verification. Existing state: file token_fuzz_test.go already exists in the auth package (untracked) and currently passes seeds + the table-driven companion TestValidateNodeTokenProperty; it already documents the VerifyToken->ValidateNodeToken discrepancy. title: Enumerate automatic vars across the setjmp/longjmp boundary in main()'s per-test loop and render per-variable C11 7.13.2.1 verdicts
description:
Split-child 1/4 of the bf-31rd re-split (umbrella bf-31rd). Read-only analysis — NO production code change in this child.

Why this child exists: parent bf-31rd failed 3x partly because it bundled the standard-level analysis with the gcc empirical reality. This child isolates ONLY the C11 7.13.2.1 standard analysis; it does NOT touch the build or gcc.

Scope (firmware/test/test_runner.c, main()'s per-test loop region):
- Identify EVERY automatic (stack/local) variable in scope across the setjmp(g_test_jmp) -> potential longjmp() (fired inside g_tests[i].fn()) -> longjmp-return boundary. Expected set: the loop index 'i', plus confirmation that NO local passed/failed tallies exist in main() (g_failure_count and g_test_jmp are file-scope statics, NOT automatic, so 7.13.2.1 does not apply to them at all).
- For each automatic variable, state explicitly: (a) is it written between the setjmp() call and a longjmp() that could fire inside the body? (b) is it read in the post-longjmp path? For 'i': written ONLY by the for-init (i=0) and the for-increment (i++), both of which run OUTSIDE the setjmp->longjmp window (the increment runs AFTER control resumes at the setjmp site, whether by normal return or longjmp); the body only READS i. Therefore under the standard alone, 'i' is determinate and volatile is NOT strictly required by 7.13.2.1.

Acceptance criteria:
- A comment on THIS bead (br comment) lists every automatic variable in scope across the boundary, gives each a written/not-written and read/not-read verdict, and states the per-variable 7.13.2.1 conclusion.
- Conclusion explicitly notes that, per the standard alone, 'i' does not require volatile — and flags that this is ONLY the standard-level finding (the gcc -Wclobbered reality is child 2's scope).
- No change to any file under firmware/ in this child.

comments.author: cli
comments.text:
## C11 7.13.2.1 audit — automatic vars across the setjmp/longjmp boundary in main()'s per-test loop

**Child 1/4 of the bf-31rd re-split. Standard-level analysis ONLY.** No file under `firmware/` was modified in this child (verified: `git status` of `firmware/test/test_runner.c` is unchanged by this bead; the existing in-source audit comment at `test_runner.c:308-341` is left untouched). The gcc `-Wclobbered` empirical reality is **out of scope** here — it is child 2's scope. This comment renders only what C11 7.13.2.1 alone says.

### The boundary under audit

`firmware/test/test_runner.c`, `main()`, per-test loop body (lines 239-345):

```c
for (volatile int i = 0; i < g_test_count; i++) {
    printf("RUN: %s\n", g_tests[i].name);
    if (setjmp(g_test_jmp) == 0) {     // <-- setjmp() invocation
        g_tests[i].fn();               // <-- potential longjmp() fires inside here
    }                                  // <-- post-longjmp fall-through path
}
```

The boundary is: the `setjmp(g_test_jmp)` call → a `longjmp()` that fires inside `g_tests[i].fn()` → the longjmp-return path at the `setjmp` call site.

### Enumeration of automatic variables in scope across the boundary

A walk of `main()`'s frame yields exactly **one** automatic object whose scope spans the boundary:

| # | Object | Storage | Scope across boundary? |
|---|--------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | `i` (`volatile int`, for-init, line 239) | **automatic** (block-scoped) | **YES** — scope is the whole loop body incl. the setjmp call, the body call, and the post-longjmp fall-through |

**Confirmation: NO local passed/failed tallies exist in `main()`.** The only counters/state touched by the recovery flow live outside `main()`'s frame:
- `g_failure_count` — file-scope `static` (line 143), bumped inside `test_record_failure()` (line 170), **not** an automatic of `main()`.
- `g_test_jmp` — file-scope `static` (line 132), **not** an automatic of `main()`.
- `g_tests`, `g_test_count` — file-scope `static` (lines 73-74), not automatics of `main()`.

Both statics have **static storage duration**, so 7.13.2.1 (which speaks only to objects of automatic storage duration) **does not apply to them at all** — their values are unconditionally preserved across `longjmp`. No written/read verdict is required for them under the standard; they are listed only to confirm they are **not** automatics and thus outside the clobber rule's reach. `qsort`/`printf`/`setjmp` arguments are expressions/temporaries, not named automatics, and `qsort` runs before the loop regardless.

### Per-variable verdict — `i`

**The three conjunctive conditions of C11 7.13.2.1p3** (an automatic is indeterminate on longjmp return only if ALL hold): (1) automatic storage duration, local to the function containing the `setjmp`; (2) non-volatile-qualified type; (3) changed between the `setjmp` invocation and the `longjmp` call.

**(a) Written between the `setjmp()` call and a `longjmp()` fired inside the body? — NO.**
The only writes to `i` are the for-init `i = 0` (runs once at loop entry, **before** any `setjmp` call) and the for-increment `i++` (runs at the **end** of each iteration, **after** the body). Within one iteration the order is `setjmp()` → body-or-longjmp-return → end of loop body → `i++`. When a `longjmp` fires inside `g_tests[i].fn()`, control transfers **directly** to the `setjmp` return site; `i++` does **not** execute between the `setjmp()` call and the `longjmp()`. It executes only **after** control has already resumed at the `setjmp` site. Inside the body, `i` is only **read** (to index `g_tests[i]`), never written.

**(b) Read in the post-longjmp path? — YES.**
On longjmp return, `setjmp` yields non-zero, the `if` body is skipped, control falls through to the end of the for-body, where `i++` (reads `i`, then writes) and the `i < g_test_count` re-test (reads `i`) both execute.

**7.13.2.1 conclusion for `i`:** condition (3) — "changed between the `setjmp` invocation and the `longjmp` call" — is **not met** (no write to `i` lands in that window). The conjunctive rule therefore does not bite: **`i` is determinate on the longjmp-return path.** Condition (2) (the `volatile` qualifier on line 239) is consequently **not needed** to keep `i` determinate under the standard — the "changed between setjmp and longjmp" failure alone is sufficient.

### Standard-level conclusion (this child's entire scope)

**Per C11 7.13.2.1 alone, `i` does NOT require `volatile`.** It is determinate on the longjmp-return path purely because it is not changed between the `setjmp()` call and a `longjmp()` fired inside `g_tests[i].fn()` — the for-init runs before `setjmp` and the for-increment runs after the body resumes. No automatic in `main()`'s frame meets all three conjuncts of 7.13.2.1p3, and the only statics in play (`g_failure_count`, `g_test_jmp`) are outside the rule's scope by storage duration.

⚠ **Scope flag:** the above is ONLY the standard-level finding. Whether `volatile` is nevertheless **kept** on `i` to satisfy gcc's `-Wclobbered` (a heuristic warning that does not track the "changed between setjmp and longjmp" distinction) is a separate, empirical/compiler question and is **deliberately deferred to child 2** of this re-split. This child asserts nothing about gcc and changes nothing under `firmware/`.

comments.author: cli
comments.text:
## C11 7.13.2.1 enumeration — automatics across the setjmp/longjmp boundary in main()'s per-test loop

Scope: firmware/test/test_runner.c, main()'s per-test loop. This is split-child 1/4 of the bf-31rd re-split. READ-ONLY, standard-level only — no file under firmware/ touched, and no gcc/-Wclobbered analysis here (that is child 2's scope).

The boundary under audit:

```
for (volatile int i = 0; i < g_test_count; i++) {
    printf("RUN: %s\n", g_tests[i].name);          // before setjmp, unaffected
    if (setjmp(g_test_jmp) == 0) {                  // <-- setjmp() call
        g_tests[i].fn();                            // <-- longjmp() can fire in here
    }                                               //     (via test_record_failure -> longjmp(g_test_jmp,1))
}                                                   // on longjmp return: fall through if (no else),
                                                    // then i++ and i < g_test_count re-test
```

Complete set of automatic (stack/local) variables in scope across the boundary: **{ i }** — and nothing else.

### Variable: `i` — automatic, `volatile int`, loop index
- (a) Written between the setjmp() call and a longjmp() fired inside the body? **NO — not written.** The only writes to `i` are (1) the for-init `i = 0`, which runs once before the loop and before any in-iteration setjmp, and (2) the for-increment `i++`, which runs AFTER control resumes at the setjmp call site — i.e. after the body has already returned, whether normally or via longjmp. Both writes therefore land OUTSIDE the [setjmp() call -> longjmp() firing inside g_tests[i].fn()] window. Within that window the body only READS `i` (to index `g_tests[i]`); no write to `i` occurs between the setjmp() call and a longjmp() that could fire inside `g_tests[i].fn()`.
- (b) Read in the post-longjmp path? **YES — read.** On a longjmp return, setjmp yields non-zero, control falls through the `if` (no else body) into the loop continuation, where `i++` reads-and-writes `i` and the `i < g_test_count` condition reads `i`. These post-longjmp reads require `i` to be determinate.
- **7.13.2.1 verdict — DETERMINATE; volatile NOT strictly required by the standard.** The standard renders an automatic object indeterminate on longjmp return ONLY if ALL THREE hold: (1) local to the function containing setjmp, (2) non-volatile, AND (3) modified between the setjmp() call and the longjmp() call. For `i`:
  - (1) local to main() — YES;
  - (2) non-volatile — NO (`i` is `volatile`-qualified, which alone exempts it from the indeterminate rule);
  - (3) modified between setjmp() and longjmp() — NO (see (a) above).
  Two of the three conditions fail, so `i` is determinate on the longjmp-return path under the standard alone. Per 7.13.2.1, `i` does NOT require `volatile`.

### No other automatics in scope — confirmed
- **No local passed/failed tallies exist in main().** main() declares no counters of its own; its body holds only the loop index `i`. The qsort() call and the setjmp()/printf()/fn() calls are expressions, not automatic declarations.
- **`g_failure_count` is file-scope `static` (declared outside main(), bumped inside test_record_failure() before the longjmp) — NOT automatic, so 7.13.2.1 does not apply to it at all.**
- **`g_test_jmp` is file-scope `static` — NOT automatic, 7.13.2.1 does not apply.**
- (Likewise `g_tests[]` and `g_test_count` are file-scope statics, not in scope of the rule.)

### Standard-level conclusion
Per C11 7.13.2.1 alone, the sole automatic across the boundary — `i` — is determinate after a longjmp return (it is not modified in the setjmp->longjmp window, and it is volatile-qualified regardless), so **`volatile` is NOT strictly required by the standard.** This is ONLY the standard-level finding: the empirical gcc `-Wclobbered` reality (why `volatile` is kept on `i` anyway) is explicitly child 2's scope and is out of bounds for this child. No file under firmware/ was modified.

title: Wrap each test in per-test setjmp/longjmp recovery so a failing assertion is non-fatal
description:
Split-child 2 of 3 for parent bf-1na (Add main() entry-point skeleton with setjmp recovery loop and run-command header). Builds on child 1 (bf-1zew, which added the run-command comment + the naive registry iteration that calls each g_tests[i].fn() directly). This child is the core contract of bf-1na: make a failing assertion non-fatal by driving each test through the per-test setjmp/longjmp recovery loop.

Scope (extend firmware/test/test_runner.c):
- In main()'s per-test loop, before calling the body, setjmp(g_test_jmp) — the file-scope jmp_buf from sibling bf-3id. Evaluate its return: on the DIRECT call setjmp returns 0 and the body runs normally; if a failed assertion inside the body calls test_record_failure(), that longjmp(g_test_jmp, 1) returns control to the setjmp call site with a non-zero value instead.
- On the non-zero (longjmp) return, do NOT re-invoke the body — just fall through to advance the loop. On the 0 return, call g_tests[i].fn() as before.
- Either way the loop advances to i+1, so a failure in test N never blocks tests N+1..end. That per-test isolation is the whole point.
- Keep printing one observable line per test BEFORE the setjmp (e.g. the RUN: line from child 1) so that, when a test fails, the lines for the tests after it still appear — which is how this child's acceptance is verified. (Outcome-based PASS/FAIL labels land in child 3; do not add them here.)

Implementation note (the classic setjmp trap): any automatic variable that is modified between setjmp and longjmp AND read after the longjmp returns must be volatile-qualified, else its value is indeterminate (C11 7.13.2.1). The loop index i is read in the post-longjmp path — confirm it is not modified between the setjmp call and the longjmp (it is only incremented by the for-loop increment, which runs AFTER control returns), so no volatile is needed; double-check this holds for the structure you write.

Acceptance criteria:
- main() setjmp()s into g_test_jmp before each test and calls the body only on the 0 return; on a longjmp return it advances without re-calling.
- A failing assertion in one test does NOT prevent later tests from running — verifiable by temporarily adding an intentionally-failing TEST() followed by a passing TEST() and confirming both tests' per-test lines print and the process completes (rather than aborting) when run via `make -C firmware/test test`.
- Compiles cleanly with gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra (no setjmp-related warnings).
- No changes to firmware/main/* or firmware/CMakeLists.txt.

title: Sort registered tests by name for deterministic run order
description:
Child 2 of the bf-bq9 sub-split (parent bf-bq9; grandparent bf-2i4). Depends on child 1 (bf-1na: main skeleton + setjmp loop).

Scope (extend firmware/test/test_runner.c main()):
- Add a static comparator test_entry_cmp(const void *a, const void *b) that strcmp()s two test_entry_t by their .name field.
- At the top of main(), before the per-test loop, call qsort(g_tests, (size_t)g_test_count, sizeof(g_tests[0]), test_entry_cmp) so iteration order is deterministic by name. Rationale: the C standard does NOT guarantee constructor order across translation units (within one TU it follows definition order, but across TUs and across the Makefile's test_*.c glob — which feeds TUs in a glob-dependent order — it is implementation- and link-defined). An unsorted run would order tests however gcc happened to receive them, making a failing run's interleaved PASS/FAIL output non-reproducible. Sorting by name makes it stable, which is what CI log diffing wants.

Acceptance criteria:
- Registered tests execute in ascending name-sorted order, deterministically, independent of registration or link order.
- Compiles cleanly with gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra; the suite still runs and exits 0 when all tests pass.
